title: Strategic HRM: Best-Fit, Best-Practice & AMO
simple_html:
Strategic HRM (SHRM) asks a simple question with big consequences: how should we manage people so that it actually helps the business win? Three influential answers compete — and a fourth idea, AMO, explains why good people management lifts performance.

Best-fit

Best-fit says there is no single right way to manage people. The best HR practices are the ones that fit your situation — your competitive strategy, industry, size and life stage. A low-cost retailer and a luxury brand should manage people differently because they compete differently.

Best-practice

Best-practice argues the opposite: certain "high-performance" practices — careful selection, good training, fair pay-for-performance, job security, sharing information, teamwork — raise performance almost everywhere. Adopt the proven bundle and you'll do better.

AMO: the engine

The AMO model explains the mechanism. People perform well when they have:

  • Ability — the skills to do the job (from selection and training),
  • Motivation — the will to do it (from rewards, recognition, fair treatment),
  • Opportunity — the chance to contribute (from job design, involvement, voice).

Performance needs all three; remove any one and effort drops.

Key takeaways

  • Best-fit = tailor HR to strategy and context.
  • Best-practice = adopt the universally effective bundle.
  • AMO = Ability + Motivation + Opportunity = performance.
  • The debate matters because it tells you whether to copy others or customize.
intermediate_html:
Strategic Human Resource Management (SHRM) studies how to align people management with organizational strategy so that HR contributes to competitive advantage. This lesson covers the three main schools of thought — best-fit (contingency), best-practice (universalist), and the configurational/bundles view — and the AMO model that explains how HR practices translate into performance.

1. Best-fit (contingency) perspective

Best-fit holds that HR practices must align with context to add value. Two famous variants exist. The external/strategic fit view links HR systems to the competitive strategy: Schuler & Jackson (1987) map Porter's strategies (cost, quality, innovation) to required employee behaviours and matching HR practices. The life-cycle fit view aligns HR with the organization's stage (start-up, growth, maturity, decline). Strength: it respects context and avoids one-size-fits-all. Weakness: it can be deterministic, treating strategy as fixed and downplaying employees' own interests.

2. Best-practice (universalist) perspective

Best-practice claims a set of "high-performance work practices" (HPWPs) raises performance regardless of context. Pfeffer (1998) proposed seven: employment security, selective hiring, self-managed teams, high contingent pay, extensive training, reduced status differences, and information sharing. Huselid's (1995) landmark study found bundles of such practices were associated with lower turnover and higher productivity and firm value. Strength: clear, evidence-backed prescription. Weakness: which list is "the" list varies, and practices that work in one culture/sector may not transfer.

3. Configurational / bundles view

This middle position stresses internal (horizontal) fit: it is not single practices but coherent bundles that work, because practices reinforce one another (MacDuffie, 1995). Selective hiring is wasted without training; pay-for-performance is unfair without good appraisal. The configurational view seeks an ideal internally consistent system that also fits strategy — combining horizontal and vertical fit.

4. The AMO model — the linking mechanism

Why do HPWPs raise performance? The AMO framework (Appelbaum et al., 2000) proposes that individual performance (P) is a function of three components: P = f(Ability, Motivation, Opportunity).

  • Ability — knowledge and skills, built through selective recruitment and training.
  • Motivation — willingness to exert effort, shaped by reward, recognition, job security and procedural fairness.
  • Opportunity — structures that let employees apply their ability and motivation: job design, autonomy, teamwork, involvement and voice.

HR practices are bundled to feed all three levers. Crucially, the relationship is multiplicative-like: high ability and motivation are wasted if there is no opportunity to contribute, which is why empowerment and involvement matter as much as skills and pay.

5. Worked example

A manufacturer suffers high defects and low productivity. An AMO diagnosis reveals: operators are skilled (Ability OK), pay is competitive (Motivation OK), but rigid supervision means workers cannot stop the line or suggest fixes (Opportunity weak). The leverage is not more training or pay but redesigning work for involvement — quality circles, problem-stopping authority, and a suggestion scheme. This pinpoints AMO's diagnostic power: invest where the binding constraint is.

6. How to apply it

  • Use AMO as a diagnostic: for any performance gap, ask which lever (A, M or O) is the constraint.
  • Use best-fit to ensure vertical alignment with strategy.
  • Use the bundles logic to ensure horizontal coherence — design practices as a reinforcing system, not a shopping list.
  • Recognize the "black box": AMO is the bridge between HR practices and outcomes the next lessons build on.

This connects forward to Performance Management and Reward Management, which operationalize the Motivation lever, and to Learning & Development, which builds Ability.
full_html:
Strategic Human Resource Management (SHRM) concerns the patterns of planned HR deployments intended to enable an organization to achieve its goals (Wright & McMahan, 1992). The field is organized around a long-running theoretical contest — universalism (best-practice) versus contingency (best-fit) versus configuration (bundles) — and around the AMO framework that opens the "black box" linking HR systems to performance. This lesson develops each rigorously, with the seminal evidence, debates and a worked diagnosis.

1. Theoretical foundations

SHRM rests on the resource-based view (Barney, 1991): advantage derives from resources that are valuable, rare, inimitable and non-substitutable. Because the HR system and the human/social capital it generates are causally ambiguous and socially complex, they are difficult to imitate (Wright, Dunford & Snell, 2001). The question SHRM answers is how to configure that system. Delery & Doty (1996) formalized three logics — universalistic, contingency and configurational — which remain the field's organizing map.

2. Best-practice / universalism

The universalist claim is that some HR practices are good for all organizations. Pfeffer's (1998) seven practices — employment security, selective hiring, self-managed teams/decentralization, comparatively high and contingent pay, extensive training, reduced status barriers, and information sharing — exemplify the "high-performance work system" (HPWS). The empirical cornerstone is Huselid (1995): in a sample of nearly 1,000 US firms, one standard-deviation increase in HPWP use was associated with markedly lower turnover, higher sales per employee and substantial increases in market value. Subsequent meta-analyses (e.g., Combs et al., 2006) confirm a positive, economically meaningful HPWS–performance association. Critiques: the lists differ across authors (the "which practices?" problem); studies are largely cross-sectional, raising reverse-causality concerns (profitable firms can afford better HR); and universalism underweights national-culture and institutional differences (Boxall & Purcell, 2016).

3. Best-fit / contingency

Contingency theory holds that HR effectiveness depends on alignment with moderating variables — above all competitive strategy. Schuler & Jackson (1987), building on Porter (1985), specify the "needed role behaviours" for cost, quality and innovation strategies and the HR practices that elicit them: e.g., innovation requires long-term, group-based, developmental, risk-tolerant systems, whereas cost leadership requires results-oriented, efficiency-focused, short-term controls. Miles & Snow (1984) offer a parallel typology (defenders vs prospectors). The life-cycle variant aligns HR to organizational stage. Critiques: it can be overly deterministic, assuming strategy is given and rational; it struggles with multiple, simultaneous competitive demands (e.g., cost and quality); and like universalism it tends to be unitarist, treating employee interests as derivative of strategy rather than legitimate in themselves (Legge, 1995).

4. Configurational / bundles

The configurational logic emphasizes internal (horizontal) fit: synergistic bundles outperform isolated practices because of complementarities. MacDuffie's (1995) study of automotive assembly plants showed that "flexible production" plants combining work-system, HR-policy and bundle coherence achieved superior productivity and quality — the practices worked because they fit each other and the production logic. Configuration seeks an internally consistent ideal type that also achieves vertical fit, integrating both alignment dimensions. The trade-off is "deadly combinations" — practices that individually help but conflict in combination (e.g., individual incentive pay alongside team-based work).

5. The AMO model: opening the black box

The "black box" problem (Boselie, Dietz & Boon, 2005) is that even where HR practices correlate with outcomes, the causal pathway is obscure. The AMO framework (Appelbaum, Bailey, Berg & Kalleberg, 2000) provides the dominant answer: performance arises when employees have the Ability to perform, the Motivation to perform, and the Opportunity to participate. HPWPs map onto these three: ability-enhancing practices (rigorous selection, training); motivation-enhancing practices (performance-related pay, recognition, security, fairness, career development); and opportunity-enhancing practices (involvement, autonomous teams, voice, information sharing). Performance is best understood as conjunctive: P = f(A, M, O) with all three necessary — a workforce that is able and willing but structurally prevented from contributing (low O) will underperform, which is why opportunity/voice is integral rather than optional.

6. Worked diagnosis

A call-centre with high attrition and poor service applies an AMO audit. Ability: agents are well-selected and trained — adequate. Motivation: pay is fine, but rigid scripts, surveillance metrics and no recognition erode willingness — weak. Opportunity: agents cannot deviate from scripts to solve unusual problems and have no channel to flag recurring issues — weak. The evidence-based intervention bundles motivation and opportunity levers: discretion within guardrails, recognition for resolution quality, and a structured voice mechanism feeding process fixes. Note the bundle logic — adding discretion (O) without rewarding good judgement (M) would create risk without payoff. This illustrates why single-practice fixes commonly fail.

7. Debates and common mistakes

  • Causality and the "so what" of correlation: cross-sectional HPWS studies cannot prove direction; longitudinal and mechanism-based (AMO) work is the stronger evidentiary base.
  • Unitarism: most SHRM assumes harmonious interests; pluralist and critical scholars argue this masks the wage-effort bargain and the role of collective voice and can legitimize work intensification.
  • Treating fit as static: strategies and environments change; fit must be dynamic, requiring HR flexibility (Wright & Snell, 1998).
  • "Pick a side": the schools are complementary in practice — use best-fit for vertical alignment, configuration for horizontal coherence, and AMO as the diagnostic mechanism.
  • Ignoring implementation: it is the HR practices as experienced by employees (intended vs actual vs perceived) that drive outcomes — line-manager enactment matters (Purcell & Hutchinson, 2007).

8. Cross-links

This lesson is the analytical hub of the course. The Ability lever is developed in Learning & Development (Lesson 5) and HR Planning, Recruitment & Selection (Lesson 3); the Motivation lever in Performance Management (Lesson 4) and Reward Management (Lesson 6); the Opportunity lever in Employee Relations & Engagement (Lesson 7). The evidence-and-measurement debate is resolved with data in Talent, Wellbeing & HR Analytics (Lesson 8). Mastery means being able to diagnose any performance problem through AMO while testing the chosen practices for both vertical and horizontal fit.
